Hiring for Information Architect @ Iselin, New Jersey for a contract position on W2.

Interested candidates can send your resumes to satya@mind-wareinc.com and call me @ 615-964-4775

Description:
Seeking an experienced Information Architect to design and implement enterprise knowledge graphs and semantic data structures to power AI-driven solutions, requiring expertise in metadata management, schema design, ontology creation, and data integration across systems.

Key Responsibilities: Design ontologies, taxonomies, semantic schemas; build scalable knowledge graphs; map and normalize data across heterogeneous systems; define metadata standards and entity mappings; ensure compliance and governance alignment; architect data flows for RAG, semantic search, and LLM apps; integrate knowledge graph structures with OpenAI, LangGraph, Azure Cognitive Search, and other frameworks; optimize data indexing and embeddings; develop reference architectures and best practices; collaborate with architects and SMEs; define lifecycle management for schemas and ontologies; evaluate emerging tools/technologies; conduct POCs for scalable architectures.

Required Qualifications: Bachelor s/Master s in Information Science, Computer Science, Data Engineering, or related; 5+ years in information architecture/semantic modeling; proven experience in knowledge graphs, ontologies, semantic schemas; expertise in metadata management, schema harmonization, and multi-application integration; experience with Azure Cognitive Search, Data Lake, Synapse, Azure AI; hands-on with OpenAI APIs, LangGraph/LangChain, vector databases, AI graph frameworks; proficiency with Neo4j, Stardog, Azure Cosmos DB Graph, RDF/SPARQL; familiarity with embeddings, RAG pipelines, LLM search/reasoning.

Preferred Skills: Experience with enterprise KG tools (TigerGraph, Stardog, Anzo, GraphQL); knowledge of data governance (GDPR, SOX, etc.); strong communication bridging technical and business; prior work in large-scale AI/ML or digital transformation.

Agenic AI is where all the power is, but it comes with a lot of cost.  Is the cost worth it?  That’s the worry I have after seeing some of these use cases and implementations.  Obviously the AI bandwagon is here and everyone can benefit from being on it.  But my concern is the amount of companies hiring AI implementation experts who aren’t weighing the cost.  How are consultants sleeping at night knowing they’re implementing an AI API solution that costs 15cents a call when there is a perfectly working zapier option that costs half a penny per call.

This reminds me of consulting in the days of WEB 2.0 and having to have the tough conversations with CEO’s and educate them on the things that “Can” be done but might not necessarily “Need” to be done.  At least not yet.  In my opinion, it’s the ethical duty of every consultant to prioritize corporate responsibility over contract security.  If you don’t know the cost of what you’re doing then you can bet your ass the client doesn’t either.

How do you align AI in a world of plural, conflicting, and evolving human values?

A starting point is human society itself.

Sydney Levine and I are hiring a postdoc at New York University to combine insights from cultural evolution, computational moral cognition, and AI safety.

Human societies solved an extraordinary version of the multi-agent alignment problem: getting millions, eventually billions, of people to cooperate, coordinate, and enforce shared norms. Those solutions were built over generations through culture, institutions, moral systems, and the cognitive machinery that supports them.

Now we are building AI systems that face many of the same problems, only faster, stranger, and with fewer inherited constraints.

As a postdoc, you will work with us and our networks to use ideas from cultural evolution and moral cognition to think more clearly about AI alignment, cooperation, norm formation, negotiation, and value conflict in multi-agent systems.

To offer a glimpse of the kind of questions and approaches you would be working on, consider the question of how the mechanisms of cooperation operate at scale, how are they made concrete in moral norms, and can analogous mechanisms be engineered into multi-agent AI systems? You would answer this question by building and testing computational models, developing multi-agent simulations where agents, aligned to different users’ values, must coordinate, negotiate, and resolve conflicts, and designing and testing methods for aligning individual AI agents to user values in ways that reflect how humans transmit those values and align to one another. These synchronic models of moral cognition will be complemented by diachronic models of how moral consensus emergence and expands over cultural evolutionary time. The diversity of human values are themselves continually evolving - how might they coevolve with AI agents?

We are looking for someone with a PhD (or are close to completion) in computer science, computational cognitive science, or any quantitative science, and a genuine interest in working across disciplinary boundaries. We’re especially excited about candidates with backgrounds or serious interest in one or more of: computational modeling of social learning, norms, or moral cognition; cultural evolution and gene-culture coevolution; evolutionary game theory and the evolution of cooperation; mechanism design and institutional design; cooperative AI and multi-agent systems; the study and development of large language models; cross-cultural psychology and large-scale behavioral data; philosophy of morality or social contract theory. You don’t need all of these disciplinary backgrounds, but you should be someone who finds it natural to move between individual cognition, population dynamics, and engineered systems.

The role is based in New York, starting this summer.

Here is what I believe is changing in high-tech M&A, based on how AI is affecting sourcing, diligence, and PMI:
1. Sourcing is getting smarter
AI is helping teams identify potential targets earlier by analyzing signals such as patent activity, hiring patterns, earnings commentary, and market moves. That gives acquirers a better chance to spot opportunities before a company is formally in play.
2. Due diligence is getting faster
AI is accelerating document review, contract analysis, risk flagging, and initial Q&A development. It is not replacing diligence judgment, but it is reducing manual effort and helping teams focus faster on the issues that matter most.
3. PMI is becoming more data-driven
The handoff from diligence to integration has historically been one of the biggest sources of value leakage. AI is starting to improve that transition by helping teams map org structures, identify system overlaps, track synergy risks, and monitor execution more dynamically after close.
Another important shift: AI compliance readiness is becoming a more visible diligence and valuation factor in tech deals, especially in cross-border situations.
AI will not replace judgment in M&A. But it is quickly becoming the operating layer that separates faster, better-prepared acquirers from everyone else. The bigger question is no longer whether firms will use AI in deals — it is where they will trust it, and where they still should not.
The firms building these capabilities now are not just moving faster. They are reshaping how competitive advantage is created in deal-making.
For those interested in going deeper, two useful references are Deloitte’s 2025 M&A Generative AI Study and Kison Patel’s recent content on practical AI tools and use cases in M&A.
How are you seeing AI show up most meaningfully in M&A today — sourcing, diligence, or integration?

Do temporary contracts discourage firms from training workers? Our new paper says yes, and shows that policy can change this.

With Jonas Jessen (WZB Berlin Social Science Center) and Carlos Victoria Lanzón (Universidad Complutense de Madrid), we study Spain's latest labour market reform, which sharply restricted temporary hiring. 

We use 3.1 million job postings around the reform to show that:

💥 Hiring shifted rapidly from temporary to permanent contracts

💥 Firm-provided training in job postings rose 4.3 percentage points (53%) in occupations most affected by the reform

💥 The pre-reform training gap across occupations with high and low reliance on temporary contracts was fully closed within two years

The logic is straightforward. Training is an upfront cost with returns that accrue over time. So when firms expect workers to stay longer, they have stronger incentives to invest in training.

This matters well beyond Spain, as many countries have high shares of temporary employment. At a time when AI is reshaping skill requirements, understanding what drives firms to invest in training is especially important.

Current US Recruitment Scenario (2026)

1. Shift from Volume Hiring → Skills-Based Hiring
Companies are moving away from “years of experience”,.
Strong focus on skills, certifications, and real capabilities
“Skills over degree” hiring is rapidly increasing

What it means for recruiters:-
Boolean searches now prioritize skills + tools (Terraform, Azure, AI, etc.)
Reduced dependence on job titles alone

2. AI is Transforming Recruitment
AI tools are now handling:
Resume screening
Interview scheduling
Candidate communication
Up to 80% of repetitive tasks can be automated

Reality check: Recruiters are evolving into strategic advisors, not just sourcers.

3. Decline in Entry-Level Hiring
AI is reducing demand for junior roles (especially in tech & operations)
Fewer openings → Higher competition

Impact:
Companies prefer mid-level or highly skilled talent
Campus hiring is becoming more specialized

4. Rise of Contract & Contingent Hiring
Increased focus on:
Contract roles
Project-based hiring
Staff augmentation
Full-time hiring has slowed

5. Quality over Speed Hiring Model

Earlier focus: Speed & volume submissions
Current focus: Quality profiles & right match

Scenario:
Misaligned JDs lead to early attrition

Hiring managers expect:
Fewer resumes
Highly relevant candidates

6. Tech & Niche Skills Talent Shortage
High demand for:
Cloud (Azure, AWS)
AI/ML
Cybersecurity
DevOps

Ground reality:
Companies struggle to find qualified talent, not applicants

What Clients Expect from Recruiters in 2026
Strong niche talent pipelines
Faster turnaround with quality profiles
Real-time market insights (salary, availability)
Strong screening, not just sourcing

I saw a job posting looking for a “Prompt Engineer”…
…with experience in distributed systems, APIs, MLOps, security, and product.

That’s not a role. That’s a team.

And here’s the uncomfortable truth:
We’re not hiring prompt engineers anymore. We’re just calling them that.
Because building AI agents that actually work in production has very little to do with prompts. It’s systems engineering.

The real skill stack looks like this:
> System design - agents are distributed systems, not chatbots
>  Tool & contracts - vague schemas = production hallucinations
>  Retrieval (RAG) - your agent is only as good as its context
>  Reliability - retries, fallbacks, circuit breakers (yes, like backend)
>  Security - prompt injection is not theoretical
>  Evaluation - “it feels better” is not a metric
>  Product thinking - users need to trust it, not just use it

Here’s what most teams get wrong:
They debug agents by rewriting prompts. But 9 out of 10 times, the failure isn’t in the prompt. It’s in the system.

We’re watching a role rename happen in real time: 

Prompt engineer > Agent engineer

Which begs the uncomfortable question: Is this actually a team?
Or is this simply what a baseline Senior Engineer must look like in 2026?

In the month of March, I saw 15 stage shows, and in the past few weeks I’ve had *at least* that many coffee chats. The real theatrics are in corporate America. 

We leaders who came of age in the workplace in the last decade are in our “Act 3 Fatigue.”

Act 1️⃣: The pandemic. We prioritized work while the world shut down, helping companies deliver record growth, and getting more responsibility and shiny new titles. But the social satisfaction and relationship-building that normally accompany new leadership roles were gone. 

Act 2️⃣: The layoffs. Even the ones who kept their jobs watched our friends, the people they endlessly slacked and zoom happy hour’d with, unceremoniously exited. The social contract between employers and high performers cracked, and it hasn't been repaired.

And now, we’re stuck in Act 3️⃣: AI pressure. This feels like an “improvised third act,” with no real vision or direction. Just executives who want innovation ("why can’t you just vibe code a solution to that three-year problem over the weekend?”) dropped onto teams already gassed at the end of an 8-show week. 

NOBODY WANTS AN ACT 3 IN THE FIRST PLACE! And now we’re stuck in this efficiency theatre. 

Everyone has a dozen new AI tools, yet nobody is working less. I LOVE building with AI, but for most companies I hear about, automation is happening at the individual level while the actual painful infrastructure (foundational data, procurement, annual planning, hiring workflows spread across eight disconnected systems) stays exactly as broken as it was. 

🎬 If you’re a Director+ in this environment (pun intentional): 

⭐️ Own the vision rather than mandating velocity. Assign real outcomes to real owners, then trust and empower them to deliver.

🎟️ Get your house in order before you open the doors. Real AI uplift comes from connecting broken processes across systems, not automating one step in a broken chain.

Act 3 Fatigue is real, but we still have time to write a better ending.  "Learn AI or get left behind" isn’t a people strategy, and it won’t be the deciding factor for whether or not a company wins. 

I’m eager to continue chatting with leaders and companies who are excited to put pen to paper on this!

America didn't lose the AI race to a smarter algorithm — it lost it at the visa office

The United States spent $285.9 billion building AI infrastructure in a single year, then passed a law making it unaffordable for the people who actually build AI to work there.

The talent collapse is already happening:

The number of AI researchers moving to the US dropped 89% since 2017, with 80% of that collapse occurring in the last 12 months alone. The Stanford AI Index 2026 documented this on page 200 of its 423-page report. Switzerland and Singapore now have more AI researchers per capita than the country that invented the transformer architecture. The US ranks 24th globally in actual AI adoption, behind the UAE and Singapore.

The H-1B fee killed the pipeline:

The Trump administration set the new H-1B visa cost at $100,000 per hire paid by the employer. OpenAI hiring a Tsinghua postdoc pays $100,000 before that researcher writes a single line of code. Google hiring the Indian PhD who co-authored the foundational paper their model depends on pays $100,000. The talent pipeline was not disrupted — it was legislated out of existence.

China closed a 20-point gap while America built a wall:

Two years ago US models led Chinese equivalents by more than 20 percentage points on key benchmarks. As of March 2026 Anthropic's top model leads China's best by just 2.7 points. China now leads the world in AI publications, patents, and industrial robot installations. US and Chinese models have traded the number one benchmark position multiple times since early 2025. Jensen Huang noted publicly that 50% of the world's top AI researchers are Chinese nationals.

The infrastructure means nothing without the people:

The US bought millions of Nvidia chips, signed $300 billion cloud contracts, and plans nuclear reactors to power GPU farms. None of it matters if the researchers who write the algorithms cannot enter the country. The US strategy for 20 years was to train the world's best minds at Stanford and MIT and keep them. That deal is dead.

The smart money already left:

Anthropic opened a Zurich office. OpenAI is hiring in Dublin and Singapore. DeepMind is expanding in London rather than Mountain View. These are not strategic pivots — they are evacuations. The companies cannot get talent into San Francisco so they are rebuilding pipelines in countries that still issue visas.

The lead was not lost in a lab:

When China launches a frontier model that outperforms anything built in the US, the postmortem will not blame the algorithm or the benchmark. It will trace back to a $100,000 fee and an 89% collapse in researcher immigration. The most expensive border wall in history was not built on the southern border. It was built around the people who make the technology work.

Strategic thinking. 
Platform ownership. 
Data contracts. 
Observability.

One of the most common claims about AI systems today:

“There’s a human in the loop.”

In both GDPR and the EU AI Act, that matters.

Under GDPR, decisions that are solely automated and produce legal or similarly significant effects trigger specific protections (subject to limited exceptions, such as necessity for a contract or explicit consent, with additional safeguards).

Under the EU AI Act, human oversight is a core requirement for high-risk systems.

But in practice, the question is not whether a human exists in the process.

It’s whether that involvement is meaningful.

If a human reviewer:
	•	relies primarily on the AI output
	•	lacks visibility into how the system reached its conclusions
	•	or is operating under time or structural constraints

…the oversight may be more formal than substantive.

This creates a gap between:
	•	what systems are designed to do
	•	and how they are actually used in practice

For organizations deploying AI systems particularly in areas like hiring, access to essential services, or legal decision-making—this distinction matters.

Because “human in the loop” is not, by itself, a safeguard.

Most companies say they hire for skills. Almost none of them do.

They hire for experience. Which made sense when the job stayed the same for five years. It doesn’t anymore.

The World Economic Forum estimates 59% of the global workforce will need reskilling by 2030. That’s not a future problem. That’s the people you’re hiring right now, into roles that will look completely different before the end of their first contract.

So when your process starts with a CV - filtering for familiar job titles, recognisable company names, career paths that look like the last person who did the job, you are screening for the past.

Here’s the part that should sting: the candidates you’re filtering out for not having the “right experience” are often exactly the people who could develop the skills you actually need. You're rejecting future capability because it doesn't have a familiar label yet.

We built TestGorilla's test library around exactly this problem.
- A Problem Solving test tells you how someone thinks under pressure.
- A Critical Thinking test tells you how they evaluate information when the answer isn't obvious. 
- The Rapid Cognitive Index measures how quickly someone can learn, not what they already know.
- And a Working With Generative AI test tells you whether they can operate in the environment your team is already moving into.

None of that shows up on a CV. All of it matters more than years in a role.

The question isn't 'can this person do the job today?'
It's 'can this person do the job this role becomes in 18 months?' 

If you don't know how your process answers the second question, that's worth figuring out before your next hire.
